topic: A person finds out their partner has been learning their native language
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the apartment felt heavy, pressing down on my chest until it was hard to draw a full breath. I stared at the textbook lying open on the coffee table—a dense collection of characters and inflections I didn't recognize. Every time I glanced over at Liam, who was humming softly while scrolling through his phone, a sharp, unwelcome knot tightened in my stomach. It wasn't anger, exactly; it was a profound sense of being utterly misplaced, like a guest in a house they’d never meant to occupy. I picked up the book, my fingers trembling slightly as I traced a word in my native script, the unfamiliar sounds mocking me. When he finally looked up, his expression was one of mild apology, and the forced brightness in his eyes made my throat constrict. "Just practicing," he offered, his tone too light, too casual. I didn't reply. I just turned my back, walking into the bedroom and sinking onto the edge of the bed, the weight of the unspoken felt like a physical burden on my shoulders.

[story 2]

I found myself constantly checking the sound of his voice, dissecting every syllable, searching for the familiar cadence that used to anchor me. Lately, it was shifting. There were moments—a fleeting phrase, a sudden burst of rapid-fire speech—where I’d catch a glimpse of something utterly foreign, a language I hadn't heard before. Each instance was like a tiny shard of glass scraping against my composure. I started retreating. I’d become meticulous about my schedule, ensuring I was occupied with tasks that demanded total concentration, anything to avoid the quiet moments when he was immersed in this new world. When he tried to share something he’d learned, his enthusiasm was genuine, but it landed with a dull thud. I would nod, offering empty affirmations, my gaze fixed somewhere beyond him, feeling profoundly disconnected, as if I were watching a performance I no longer understood.

[story 3]

The evening unfolded in a slow-motion tragedy. We were having dinner, a comfortable ritual that usually brought ease, but tonight, every shared word felt like navigating a minefield. Liam was animated, recounting a conversation he’d had with a classmate, and then, without any warning, he slipped into a different linguistic register, his accent subtly changing, the rhythm of his speech entirely altered. I watched his mouth move, trying to map the unfamiliar shapes of the sounds onto the memories of our shared past. A wave of sheer exhaustion washed over me. I picked at my food, the taste of it utterly absent. When he finally paused, realizing the shift, he looked genuinely concerned. "Sorry," he murmured, his voice returning to normal, but the damage was done. I simply pushed my plate away, the small clatter echoing too loudly in the quiet dining room. I couldn't meet his eyes; the sheer effort of processing this unfamiliar reality was draining every reserve I possessed.
